title: register_pas_asset does not assert is_pas_coin<T> or is_coin_registered<T> — opaque upstream errors
description: The public entry `register_pas_asset<T>` does not guard against re-registration or against T already being a regulated coin in the same treasury. The `policy::new_for_currency` call will abort with `EPolicyAlreadyExists` (PAS policy.move line 23) and `add_pas_coin_caps` will abort with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` — but neither surfaces a Hadron-owned error.
impact: Operator errors produce upstream error codes rather than Hadron's own. Debugging more painful. No bypass.
recommendation: Add `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered);` at the top of `register_pas_asset`.

title: PAS scripts import @mysten/pas — ensure version pinned
description: `scripts/src/operations/pas.ts` imports `PASClient` from `@mysten/pas`. The `@mysten/pas` TS client must match the on-chain PAS rev. A mismatch (e.g. client v0.3 + on-chain b64f0c5) could silently produce wrong template derivations or account address calculations.
impact: Out-of-band breakage if versions drift. Non-obvious.
recommendation: Pin `@mysten/pas` to an exact version in package.json (not a caret range). Document the compatibility matrix in the README. Validate at test time via `pasClient.getPackageConfig().namespaceId` — assert it matches the on-chain namespace you expect.
evidence:
import { PASClient } from "@mysten/pas";
import deployPasTokenTemplate from "../deployPasTokenTemplate";

title: pas_supply::mint does not check account.versioning matches namespace — stale account accepted
description: Upstream `pas::account::deposit_balance<C>` calls `account.versioning.assert_is_valid_version()` internally (account.move line 128). So a stale account aborts at deposit. Hadron-side does not re-check. This is acceptable because the upstream does, but if PAS refactors deposit_balance to not assert, the check is lost.
impact: Defense-in-depth gap. Currently upstream protects us.
recommendation: Add `version.check_is_valid()` (Hadron Version) at the top of `mint<T>`, `burn<T>`, `seize<T>`. The Auth proof already checked, but a belt-and-suspenders guard documents the invariant.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset passes mut TreasuryCap to PAS without using it downstream
description: `pas::policy::new_for_currency<C>(namespace: &mut Namespace, _cap: &mut TreasuryCap<C>, clawback_allowed: bool)` takes a `&mut TreasuryCap<C>` but the argument is prefixed `_cap` — signaling unused in policy.move. Confirmed in upstream L43-68: the cap is borrowed only as proof of currency ownership; no mutation happens. Passing `&mut treasury_cap` from Hadron is therefore safe but possibly surprising.
impact: None on correctness. A future PAS version might change the contract.
recommendation: No action; boundary is current. Record as validated.

title: pas_transfer checks treasury not mut — confirm approve_transfer never needs mutation
description: `approve_transfer` takes `treasury: &Treasury` (shared object reference, immutable). All four assertions (is_mint_redeem_paused, can_send, can_receive) are read-only. Since the Treasury is a shared object, using &Treasury is safer and non-contending for parallel execution.
recommendation: Keep. Note in module docstring that `approve_transfer` is read-only against the Treasury by design, allowing high-throughput transfers.

title: Treasury::Treasury struct lacks `store` ability — design choice
description: `public struct Treasury has key` (only). Without `store`, the Treasury cannot be wrapped in another struct as a field — which is intentional (institution treasuries are shared objects only). Confirmed.

title: approve_transfer uses request.data() 3 times — redundant data accessor calls
description: `approve_transfer` calls `request.data()` three times to get sender, recipient, funds. A single `let d = request.data();` binding would be more idiomatic and allow the compiler to elide redundant method calls.
recommendation: let d = request.data(); let sender = d.sender(); let recipient = d.recipient(); let amount = d.funds().value();

title: pas_supply::burn uses .data() twice — same style issue
description: Same pattern as R4-007 in burn: `let from = request.data().owner();` + `let amount = request.data().funds().value();`.
evidence:
let from = request.data().owner();
    let amount = request.data().funds().value();

title: Missing docs on hadron::pas_transfer::transfer_approval_permit visibility intent
description: `transfer_approval_permit<T>` is `public(package)`. The existing comment says 'Must live here because `internal::permit` requires the defining module'. This is accurate — PAS `internal` crate's `permit<A>()` witness requires the call to originate from A's defining module. Good doc.
recommendation: Add a brief cross-reference: 'Called by pas_admin::register_transfer_template to satisfy pas::templates::set_template_command<A>(permit<A>, ...)'.

title: Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T> binds to T via is_coin_registered — sufficient but implicit
description: Each of the four whitelist functions takes `_: &Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. Per auth.move line 42, `new_auth<P, T>` asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()`. So the Auth proof implicitly binds T to a registered coin in THIS treasury. However, the Auth proof carries only `addr: address` (auth.move line 23) — NOT the treasury_id. Hadron relies on the `Auth<P, T>` phantom pair for treasury binding (via the Sui-OTW-uniqueness argument: `TreasuryCap<T>` is unique per OTW per package). Surface this implicit guarantee for audit.
impact: None in isolation. Depends on the OTW-uniqueness invariant holding. If a coin T were somehow registered in two different treasuries (which should be impossible given TreasuryCap uniqueness), the Auth proof from one could act on the other. Within the Hadron design, this should never happen.
recommendation: Add a SECURITY docstring on `Auth<P, T>` explaining that treasury binding relies on TreasuryCap<T> uniqueness, and that any change allowing multiple treasuries to register the same T would break this invariant. Consider embedding treasury_id in Auth<P, T> as defense-in-depth.

title: register_pas_asset uses &mut Namespace from PAS — contention on a shared singleton
description: `register_pas_asset` takes `pas_namespace: &mut Namespace`. The PAS Namespace is a shared singleton (one per deployment). Every PAS registration across all Hadron institutions serializes on this object.
impact: Sequential throughput bottleneck for PAS asset registration. Registration is rare, so this is acceptable.
recommendation: Document the contention point. For high-volume issuance flows, ensure the PTB that registers PAS assets is not batched with other `&mut Namespace` consumers.

title: Templates stored via set_template_command — dynamic_field not DOF
description: Upstream `pas::templates::set_template_command<A>` uses `dynamic_field::add(&mut templates.id, key, command)` (templates.move L41). The `Command` type is copy+drop+store. Storing as DF (not DOF) is correct because Command is not a key-abilities object. Validated.

title: No e2e script test exercising transfer — autoresolve path untested
description: `full-setup.ts` demonstrates a PAS transfer via `pasClient.call.sendBalance`, but this is a runtime demo, not a test. There is no automated integration test that verifies the autoresolve template actually wires up correctly. A template-registration regression (wrong package address, wrong module name, wrong type args) would break autoresolve silently until a real user transfers.
impact: Template misconfiguration is caught only at first transfer attempt post-deploy — high-friction regression surface.
recommendation: Add a CI-runnable script test that: (1) deploys a local sui network (or forks testnet), (2) deploys Hadron + registers a PAS coin + whitelists, (3) calls sendBalance via PASClient, (4) asserts the transfer completed and `PasTransferApproved` event was emitted.

title: full-setup.ts mixes regulated and PAS flows — example becomes cluttered
description: The example demonstrates both a regulated-coin flow AND a PAS-asset flow in one script. Useful for documentation, but makes the file long and mixes concerns. A PAS-only example would be easier for partners integrating just the PAS path.
recommendation: Split into `full-setup-regulated.ts` and `full-setup-pas.ts` (and optionally keep the combined `full-setup.ts` as an integration demo).

title: hardcoded gas budget / no gas budget configuration in PAS scripts
description: The PAS operation scripts don't set an explicit gas budget. They rely on `signAndExecute` (in utils.ts) to supply a default. For complex PTBs involving multiple Move calls (e.g. deployAndRegisterPasAsset combines finalize_registration + registerPasAsset in one PTB), the default budget may be too low on mainnet under congestion.
impact: Occasional gas underestimation; easy to diagnose.
recommendation: Audit `utils.ts:signAndExecute` for a sensible default budget, and allow per-call override. Document the expected budget for the combined PTB.
evidence:
console.log(`Registering PAS asset ${coinType}...`);
    const result = unwrapResult(await signAndExecute(tx, NETWORK));
